docs.title: Universal Science Discovery Repository
docs.text:
Discovery-first open infrastructure: explicit unknowns, testable hypotheses, cross-domain links, and reproducible workflows. Git remains the source of truth; this site is a readable view of the docs/ tree.

For the full project overview and repository layout, see the README on GitHub.

docs.title: Start here
docs.text:
• Onboarding
  • Happy path — first unknown & hypothesis (Stream A)
  • Quality bar — avoid sloppy structure & reviews
  • Vision & scope and Methodology
  • Documentation map
  • Contributing (repository root) — full contributor path
  • Contributor hub (HTML in repo; run locally per dashboard README)

docs.title: Meta
docs.text:
• Developer dashboard — operational checklist (source: .planning/STATE.md)
  • GSD integration (optional maintainers) — spec-driven tooling boundaries
  • Ingest UAT (manual) — usdr-ingest smoke / optional live OAI

docs.title: Record examples (YAML)
docs.text: High-priority unknowns and active hypotheses live under the repo root, e.g. unknowns-catalog/ and hypotheses/active/. Validate locally with python scripts/validate_schemas.py.

docs.title: How to contribute
docs.text:
See CONTRIBUTING.md for mechanics (fork, branch, PR, checks).

Anti-sloppiness playbook: QUALITY_BAR.md — CI gates, review lanes, definition of done, exemplars.

docs.title: Review expectations
docs.text:
• Substantive changes to methods or findings: require at least one reviewer familiar with the domain thread (or explicit maintainer review if the team is small).
  • Docs-only: may be fast-tracked; still run CI.
  • Data or ethics-sensitive paths: require explicit review before merge.

docs.title: Communication
docs.text:
• Prefer issues for public discussion tied to a decision record.
  • Use PR descriptions to link issues, hypotheses, and prior artifacts.

docs.title: Labels (suggested)
docs.text: Maintain GitHub labels aligned to methodology. Full palette, colors, and milestone ideas: LABELS_AND_MILESTONES.md. Core set: hypothesis, methods, finding, docs, tooling, blocked, needs-evidence.

docs.title: Cross-domain bridges
docs.text:
A bridge entry captures an explicit connection between two or more scientific fields studying the same underlying phenomenon with different language, methods, or tools — and with little awareness of each other.

Bridge files live under cross-domain/ in the repository root, validated against schemas/bridge.yaml.

docs.title: Why bridges matter
docs.text:
When physics, biology, chemistry, and medicine each study the same phenomenon, they often rediscover it independently — wasting decades. Bridges make the connection explicit so that:

  • Researchers can borrow tools and vocabulary from adjacent fields.
  • Experiments that are impossible in one field become routine in another.
  • Journal siloing stops hiding obvious collaborations.

docs.title: Active bridges
docs.text: ID Fields Unknown Hypothesis b-percolation-oncology statistical-physics ↔ oncology u-tumor-containment-percolation, u-active-matter-percolation h-adaptive-therapy-percolation-threshold b-criticality-neuroscience condensed-matter ↔ neuroscience u-brain-criticality-function h-criticality-conscious-integration b-glymphatic-aging sleep-medicine ↔ neurology ↔ geroscience u-amyloid-progression-trajectory h-glymphatic-amyloid-clearance-rate b-lichen-astrobiology synthetic-biology ↔ astrobiology u-synthetic-lichen-biofabrication h-lichen-consortium-metabolic-coupling b-topology-morphogenesis mathematical-physics ↔ developmental-biology u-topological-morphogenesis h-topological-defect-morphogenesis b-grokking-criticality machine-learning ↔ statistical-physics u-grokking-phase-transition h-grokking-criticality-universality b-quantum-biology-navigation quantum-mechanics ↔ sensory-biology u-quantum-biology-decoherence h-quantum-compass-precision b-kibble-zurek-morphogenesis cosmology ↔ developmental-biology u-kibble-zurek-embryo, u-topological-morphogenesis h-kibble-zurek-polarity-scaling

docs.title: Schema
docs.text:
Each bridge requires at minimum:

Field Purpose id Stable b-... identifier title One-sentence equivalence statement status proposed / established / contested / stale fields At least two disciplines bridge_claim Precise description of the mapping

Optional enrichment fields: translation_table, cross_pollination_opportunities, communication_gap, related_unknowns, related_hypotheses, references.

Full schema: schemas/bridge.yaml in the repository root.

docs.title: Contributing a bridge
docs.text:
1. Pick two fields you suspect are studying the same thing with different vocabulary.
  2. Copy the template below into cross-domain/<field-a>-<field-b>/b-<short-name>.yaml.
  3. Run python scripts/validate_schemas.py — it now validates bridge entries automatically.
  4. Open a PR describing the connection and at least one cross_pollination_opportunity.
id: b-your-bridge-name
title: "..."
status: proposed
fields:
  - field-a
  - field-b
bridge_claim: >
  ...

docs.title: Data plan — Phase A ingestion pilot
docs.text:
Status: Phase A (specification + single-source pilot scope) Related: ARCHITECTURE.md (external corpora & ingestion program), LEGAL.md (what may be stored and how)

This document fixes one pilot external source, non-goals, a versioned record envelope for normalized metadata, and acceptance criteria for “Phase A pilot done.” Implementation jobs, databases, and UI are out of scope here (see ARCHITECTURE phasing).

docs.title: Chosen pilot source
docs.text:
Primary recommendation: arXiv via OAI-PMH, metadata records only.

  • Harvest base URL (verify before implementation): OAI endpoint documented in arXiv help (e.g. http://export.arxiv.org/oai — follow current arXiv documentation for HTTPS and any redirects).
  • Run ingest (Phase B starter, metadata only — no PDFs): pip install -r requirements-ingest.txt && pip install -e ./packages/ingest && usdr-ingest harvest --help
  • Scope: Incremental OAI from–until (or SetSpec slice) implemented via ListIdentifiers + GetRecord (oai_dc metadata) — XML metadata payloads only. No PDFs, no LaTeX source downloads, no web scraping outside OAI.
  • Why this pilot (one sentence): A single authoritative preprint OAI stream gives bounded, metadata-first ingestion with explicit alignment to LEGAL.md (metadata / OA posture, no mirrored publisher full text) while exercising the envelope and provenance fields described in ARCHITECTURE.

Contrast (not chosen for v1 pilot): An OpenAlex API or snapshot subset is also low-friction (CC0 data) but adds aggregation semantics and citation-graph breadth better suited immediately after this pilot once the envelope is proven on one OAI-shaped pipeline.

docs.title: Non-goals (Phase A pilot)
docs.text:
• Ingesting full text (PDF/HTML), mirrored paywalled content, or systematic publisher scraping.
  • Running production schedules, durability SLOs, or Phase B-scale workers + database (idempotent upsert design may be drafted elsewhere; execution is Phase B).
  • Building discovery UI (Phase C).
  • Combining multiple external catalogs in one pilot (no Crossref/OpenAlex/Unpaywall merge in-scope for Phase A).
  • Hosting derived datasets in-repo beyond what VISION_AND_SCOPE.md already allows (references and small permissive artifacts only); pilot output may live in docs/examples or a later Phase B store.

docs.title: Record envelope (v1)
docs.text: Every normalized record ingested toward the future knowledge graph MUST carry provenance compatible with ARCHITECTURE’s “common envelope.” Version 1.0.0 is defined here.

docs.title: Field table
docs.text: Field Required Type / format Description envelope_version yes string (semver) Schema version; use 1.0.0 for this definition. source_system yes string Stable name of the upstream system (e.g. arxiv_oai). source_id yes string Primary stable identifier from the source (e.g. arXiv id 2401.00001v1). source_record_url recommended string (URI) Canonical human- or API-facing URL for the record when available. retrieved_at yes string (RFC 3339 UTC) When this snapshot was obtained from the source. license_or_status yes string License or access status applicable to stored fields (e.g. arXiv-license terms — metadata via OAI; see LEGAL). jurisdiction no string Legal jurisdiction when relevant (patents, regional registries); omit for arXiv pilot. legal_note no string Short clarification (e.g. “metadata only”, “no full text stored”). title recommended string Normalized title if present in source metadata. abstract optional string Only if source terms and LEGAL.md allow retention for this record class. authors optional array of objects Minimal shape: { "name": "..." }; extend in Phase B if needed. published_at optional string (date) Source publication or announcement date when available. subjects optional array of string Categories / keywords from source. ingestion_cursor optional string Opaque checkpoint (e.g. OAI resumption token or last from timestamp) for incremental sync. content_fingerprint optional string Hash of canonical subset of fields for idempotency (Phase B).

docs.title: JSON Schema (informative)
docs.text:
Machine-validatable copy (use in CI and clients): schemas/ingestion-envelope-1.0.0.json. The fragment below mirrors it for readers who prefer inline reference.

{
  "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema",
  "$id": "https://usdr.dev/schemas/ingestion-envelope-1.0.0.json",
  "title": "USDR ingestion record envelope",
  "type": "object",
  "additionalProperties": true,
  "required": [
    "envelope_version",
    "source_system",
    "source_id",
    "retrieved_at",
    "license_or_status"
  ],
  "properties": {
    "envelope_version": {
      "type": "string",
      "pattern": "^1\\.0\\.0$"
    },
    "source_system": { "type": "string", "minLength": 1 },
    "source_id": { "type": "string", "minLength": 1 },
    "source_record_url": { "type": "string", "format": "uri" },
    "retrieved_at": { "type": "string", "format": "date-time" },
    "license_or_status": { "type": "string", "minLength": 1 },
    "jurisdiction": { "type": "string" },
    "legal_note": { "type": "string" },
    "title": { "type": "string" },
    "abstract": { "type": "string" },
    "authors": {
      "type": "array",
      "items": {
        "type": "object",
        "required": ["name"],
        "properties": { "name": { "type": "string" } },
        "additionalProperties": true
      }
    },
    "published_at": { "type": "string" },
    "subjects": { "type": "array", "items": { "type": "string" } },
    "ingestion_cursor": { "type": "string" },
    "content_fingerprint": { "type": "string" }
  }
}

docs.title: Acceptance criteria — “Phase A pilot done”
docs.text:
1. Source lock-in: Pilot source is arXiv OAI-PMH, metadata only, with this document pointing to current arXiv OAI documentation and stating “no full text in pilot.”
  2. Envelope frozen: Version 1.0.0 field table and JSON Schema above are the contract for downstream Phase B ingestion code and graph edges (per ARCHITECTURE: every graph edge cites this envelope).
  3. Legal traceability: Plan explicitly defers to LEGAL.md for prohibited content and third-party attribution; contributors re-check arXiv terms of use at implementation time.
  4. Architecture alignment: Cross-reference to ARCHITECTURE’s “External corpora & ingestion program” and Phase A wording is satisfied (this file is the accountable spec).
  5. Discoverability: DOC_MAP.md lists this plan so Phase B work stays traceable.

Optional stretch (not required to call Phase A “done”): a small exemplar JSONL (e.g. ≤20 synthetic or hand-redacted rows) checked in under docs/examples/ — arxiv-oai-metadata-sample.v1.jsonl (from package test fixtures; not a live harvest).

Implementation note: packages/ingest implements Phase B harvest toward this envelope; unit tests validate harvest output against schemas/ingestion-envelope-1.0.0.json. Keep this page aligned when the CLI or manifest schema changes.

Manual QA: UAT_INGEST.md — smoke steps and optional live OAI dry-run.

docs.title: Revision history
docs.text: Version Date Notes 1.0.0 2026-05 Initial Phase A pilot plan — arXiv OAI metadata, envelope v1.0.0

docs.title: Developer dashboard (USDR meta)
docs.text: Human- and agent-editable checklist for repository operations — not scientific output. Canonical text lives in .planning/STATE.md at the repo root.

docs.title: HTML dashboard (browser)
docs.text:
For a single web page for contributors: ordered “start here” path, links to policies and to unknowns-catalog/ / hypotheses/, plus maintainer live STATE + ROADMAP when served locally — use dashboard/index.html. The page includes optional light/dark theme, sticky section nav with scroll highlight, and loads DM Sans / JetBrains Mono (and marked for MD panels) from CDNs when online — see dashboard/README.md. From the repo root run python -m http.server 8765, then open http://localhost:8765/dashboard/.

The Cursor Canvas at canvases/Progress.canvas.tsx is optional; the HTML hub is the main browser entry for people exploring the repo. Agents: .cursor/rules/documentation-and-dashboard.mdc (always on) requires keeping docs, CHANGELOG, STATE, and this hub aligned—especially at milestones—and spot-checking http://localhost:8765/dashboard/ after hub-affecting edits.

docs.title: Editing workflow
docs.text:
1. Update state — Open .planning/STATE.md and adjust the stable sections (Last updated, Current focus, branches/PR bullets with compare URLs, shipped items, blockers, next actions).

  2. Refresh the Cursor Canvas — Constants in canvases/Progress.canvas.tsx are a snapshot mirror of STATE (Canvas cannot read disk). After editing STATE, regenerate that block:

python scripts/sync-dashboard-from-state.py
  1. Open the Canvas in Cursor — Open canvases/Progress.canvas.tsx in the editor and use Cursor’s Canvas affordance (“Open Canvas” / canvas panel beside the chat) so the dashboard renders live. If the Canvas entry point is unavailable, editing STATE plus the snippet in the canvas source still keeps the checklist useful.

docs.title: Complementary tooling
docs.text:
The GSD /gsd-progress command (via your Cursor GSD workspace setup) complements this file set: /gsd-progress answers situational workflow status; .planning/STATE.md is the narrative checklist you trim next to MkDocs policy docs.

Integrity and licensing expectations for substantive work remain in LEGAL.md, the documentation map, and Cursor rules — this dashboard stays meta (branches, docs, ingestion milestones).

docs.title: Guiding documents → repository behaviors
docs.text:
This map is the Phase 0 traceability layer. When you change a guiding document, update the corresponding behaviors (rules, templates, or this map) in the same change.

Guiding document What it governs Concrete behaviors VISION_AND_SCOPE.md Mission, boundaries, what belongs in-repo README framing; issue labels; scope checks in AGENTS and Cursor rules METHODOLOGY.md Claims vs hypotheses, evidence bars, workflows PR template checklist; finding_review and hypothesis_thread issue templates; prompt templates in prompts/ ETHICS_REPRODUCIBILITY_AND_DATA.md Data classes, privacy, reproducibility .gitignore; data/README.md; no raw restricted data in git QUALITY_BAR.md Explicit quality / anti-sloppiness playbook CI gates; review lanes; def. of done; links METHODOLOGY.md, COLLABORATION_AND_REVIEWS.md, HAPPY_PATH_FIRST_RECORDS.md COLLABORATION_AND_REVIEWS.md Contributions, review expectations CONTRIBUTING.md; PR template; branch protection notes in OPERATING_RHYTHM.md OPERATING_RHYTHM.md Cadence, versioning, when to sync rules/CI; milestone doc + contributor hub Doc/CI updates; release/tag policy; CHANGELOG, dashboard/index.html, http://localhost:8765/dashboard/ check per .cursor/rules/documentation-and-dashboard.mdc REPOSITORY_MANIFEST.md Per-path roles and governing docs Keeping structure auditable; updates when paths change ONBOARDING.md First-hour navigation Points to all core policies and work directories HAPPY_PATH_FIRST_RECORDS.md Stream A: first unknown + hypothesis YAML → PR CONTRIBUTING.md; schemas/README.md; templates/README.md; CI validates via scripts/validate_schemas.py LABELS_AND_MILESTONES.md GitHub triage vocabulary Aligns with COLLABORATION_AND_REVIEWS.md; issue templates LICENSING_NOTES.md Exceptions to default MIT Per-file or per-artifact license metadata CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md Community behavior CONTRIBUTING.md; enforcement expectations SECURITY.md Private vulnerability / sensitive data reports Automation and secrets; not scientific debate DATA_PLAN.md Phase A external metadata pilot; envelope schema Must stay consistent with ARCHITECTURE.md ingestion section and LEGAL.md before Phase B jobs land examples/README.md Checked-in synthetic ingest JSONL exemplar + regen note Traceable to DATA_PLAN.md stretch item; rows validated in packages/ingest/tests/test_envelope_schema.py UAT_INGEST.md Manual acceptance checks for usdr-ingest Implements DATA_PLAN.md QA; automated checks in packages/ingest/tests DEV_DASHBOARD.md Meta checklist: active branches, shipped items, blockers Edits .planning/STATE.md; optional sync to Canvas via scripts/sync-dashboard-from-state.py; HTML dashboard + dashboard/README.md for browser use GSD_INTEGRATION.md Optional get-shit-done / spec-driven workflows in Cursor Boundaries vs METHODOLOGY.md, DATA_PLAN.md, LEGAL.md; does not replace contributor onboarding

docs.title: Supporting files (see manifest)
docs.text: Configuration and templates (.github/, .cursor/, .markdown-link-check.json, root LICENSE, .gitignore) are indexed in REPOSITORY_MANIFEST.md so every tracked path maps to policy.

docs.title: Gaps flagged for maintainer decision
docs.text:
• License interaction: Repository default is MIT (LICENSE). Record per-artifact exceptions in LICENSING_NOTES.md when you introduce them.
  • Human subjects / regulated research: If work involves human subjects, HIPAA, or export-controlled data, add a mandatory compliance appendix and legal review before committing study protocols or data paths.
  • Branch protection: GitHub branch protection is configured in repository settings, not in git. See OPERATING_RHYTHM.md.

docs.title: Data classes
docs.text: Class May be committed Requirements Public, non-sensitive summaries Yes Provenance and license noted Derived aggregates (k-anonymous, no re-identification risk) Yes if policy allows Document transformation pipeline Identifiable or sensitive human data No in raw form Store in approved systems only; link with access procedure Third-party restricted data No without license text Reference terms of use; do not commit proprietary dumps

docs.title: Reproducibility
docs.text:
• Pin software versions when analysis is code-backed (requirements files, lockfiles, or environment notes in the relevant folder).
  • Record random seeds where stochastic processes matter.
  • For long pipelines, a short RUNBOOK in scripts/ or methods/ beats tribal knowledge.

docs.title: Human subjects
docs.text: If any work involves human participants: obtain appropriate approvals, use consent-aligned sharing, and never commit identifiers or protected health information.

docs.title: Integrity
docs.text:
• Do not fabricate data or citations.
  • Correct errors transparently (follow-up issues or PRs).

docs.title: GSD integration (optional maintainer tooling)
docs.text: GSD / get-shit-done is an optional spec-driven workflow for Cursor and similar environments: discuss → plan → execute phases with tracked artifacts (often under .planning/). USDR does not require GSD for contributors; this page is for maintainers who already use GSD and want alignment with repository policy.

docs.title: Boundaries
docs.text:
• Science and claims still follow METHODOLOGY.md, ETHICS_REPRODUCIBILITY_AND_DATA.md, and LEGAL.md. GSD plans are process metadata, not evidence for hypotheses.
  • Data and ingest must match DATA_PLAN.md and schemas/; do not bypass governance because a phase plan says so.
  • Canonical repo state for humans and agents remains .planning/STATE.md (see DEV_DASHBOARD.md). If you use GSD phases, reflect outcomes there or in the HTML contributor hub when milestones land.

docs.title: How it fits USDR
docs.text: Need Where to look Day-to-day checklist / branches DEV_DASHBOARD.md, STATE.md Cadence, versioning, CI expectations OPERATING_RHYTHM.md Doc ↔ behavior traceability DOC_MAP.md Installing GSD skills / CLI Upstream get-shit-done docs

docs.title: Forks and minimal checkouts
docs.text: Forks may omit .planning/ history or GSD config; that is fine. Optional GSD artifacts must never be required to read policy, contribute unknowns/hypotheses, or run documented tools (e.g. packages/ingest, MkDocs).

docs.title: Happy path — your first unknown & hypothesis
docs.text: Stream A: single track from clone → valid YAML → pull request, with no detours. Read METHODOLOGY.md for how claims, hypotheses, and evidence relate; LEGAL.md for what you may link (metadata, DOIs, open URLs — not paywalled full text).

docs.title: 0) One-time setup
docs.text:
From the repository root:

pip install -r scripts/requirements-validate.txt
python scripts/validate_schemas.py

You should see: OK: all hypothesis and unknown-catalog YAML files validate. If this fails, fix existing files or your environment before adding new records.

docs.title: 1) Add an unknown (research gap)
docs.text:
Goal: one file under unknowns-catalog/ that validates against schemas/unknown.yaml.

Step Action 1 Pick a folder: e.g. unknowns-catalog/high-priority/ (see unknowns-catalog/README.md). 2 New file: u-<slug>.yaml where <slug> is lowercase a-z, digits, dots, hyphens only (id must match ^u-[a-z0-9.-]+$). 3 Copy shape from unknowns-catalog/high-priority/u-dark-matter-microphysics.yaml and replace content with your gap. 4 Required fields: id, title (≥ 8 chars), status (open | partial | resolved | stale). 5 References: only doi / arxiv / url objects under references: — see schema. 6 Validate: python scripts/validate_schemas.py → must exit 0.

Optional but useful: draft the gap in prose first using templates/unknown-issue-template.md, then compress into YAML.

docs.title: 2) Add a hypothesis (linked to that unknown)
docs.text:
Goal: one file under hypotheses/active/ that validates against schemas/hypothesis.yaml and points at your u-... id.

Step Action 1 File: hypotheses/active/h-<slug>.yaml with id matching ^h-[a-z0-9.-]+$. 2 Copy shape from hypotheses/active/h-radio-axion-like-dm-constraints.yaml. 3 Required: id, title, status, created (ISO date YYYY-MM-DD). New work is usually status: draft until reviewed. 4 Link back: unknowns_addressed: [u-your-slug] using the same id as your unknown file’s id. 5 Evidence: each evidence_links[] item must include type: supporting | related | contradicting; use doi, arxiv, or url — not pasted paper bodies. 6 Cross-link unknown: in the unknown YAML, add your hypothesis id under suggested_hypotheses: (optional but keeps the graph readable). 7 Validate again: python scripts/validate_schemas.py → 0.

Use templates/hypothesis-pr-template.md in your PR description.

docs.title: 3) Open the PR
docs.text:
1. Branch from default (e.g. main when you have it).
  2. Commit both files (or unknown-only first if you prefer two small PRs).
  3. PR title example: content: add unknown u-… and hypothesis h-….
  4. Fill the repo pull request template — especially methodology and data checklist items.

Maintainers focus shape (schema, IDs, legal-safe links) first, then science (wording, evidence strength) — see COLLABORATION_AND_REVIEWS.md.

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text:
Symptom Fix additionalProperties / required errors Compare field-for-field with the seed examples; don’t add undeclared top-level keys. evidence_links errors Every link object needs type:; see schema. Unknown id / title rejected Pattern and length constraints in schemas/unknown.yaml. Validator not installed pip install -r scripts/requirements-validate.txt ## Where this fits
  • Quality bar: QUALITY_BAR.md — CI + review lanes + definition of done (mitigate sloppy merges).
  • Contributor hub: dashboard/index.html (same policies; run via local server).
  • Templates index: templates/README.md.
  • Schemas: schemas/README.md.

When Stream A is stable for your team, use parallel streams for examples, CI, and hub polish — but keep this path the single narrative “how to ship your first records.”

docs.title: Labels and milestones
docs.text: GitHub does not load label definitions from the repository automatically. Use this list when configuring labels under Issues → Labels (and optionally align Projects / Milestones).

docs.title: Recommended labels
docs.text:
Label Color suggestion Meaning hypothesis #7057ff Open scientific hypothesis or thread finding #0e8a16 Proposed or accepted finding under review methods #1d76db Protocols, analysis plans, reproducibility docs #0075ca Documentation and governance only tooling #d876e3 CI, bots, templates, automation enhancement #a2eeef Feature / improvement (non-science) bug #d73a4a Incorrect or broken content/tooling needs-evidence #fbca04 More data or analysis required blocked #b60205 Waiting on external dependency good first issue #7057ff Friendly for newcomers

Names match COLLABORATION_AND_REVIEWS.md. Adjust the palette to taste; keep names stable so templates stay meaningful.

docs.title: Optional milestones
docs.text: Milestone Use Backlog Triaged but not scheduled Active inquiry Hypotheses with ongoing work Evidence review Findings awaiting critique Release candidate Snapshot tag / external share imminent

docs.title: Issue template ↔ label hints
docs.text:
• Finding review template suggests finding.
  • Methods change suggests methods.
  • Hypothesis thread suggests hypothesis.
  • Bug report suggests bug.
  • Feature request suggests enhancement.

After creating labels once, you can set default labels per template in the template YAML if you switch templates to the form format later.

docs.title: Licensing notes
docs.text: The default license for this repository is the MIT License.

docs.title: When to add exceptions
docs.text:
Document exceptions here when:

  • Specific files use a different license (for example, adapted third-party text or figures).
  • Outputs (preprints, reports, datasets) should be CC BY or another public license distinct from MIT for clarity.
  • Models or weights you redistribute carry their own license terms.

docs.title: Per-artifact metadata
docs.text:
For each licensable artifact, prefer a short header or sidecar note:

  • SPDX license identifier where applicable
  • Attribution for derived work
  • Link to upstream terms of use

docs.title: Default until amended
docs.text: If this file contains only this section, MIT applies repository-wide with no recorded exceptions.

docs.title: Evidence bar
docs.text:
Before labeling work as a finding:

  1. State the hypothesis and the falsification criteria (what would convince you the hypothesis is wrong).
  2. List data sources and versions; link protocols in methods/ or equivalent.
  3. Describe analysis steps enough that another researcher could critique or reproduce the logic (code or pseudocode as appropriate).
  4. List limitations, confounders, and alternative explanations.

docs.title: Claims discipline
docs.text:
• Separate results (what was observed or computed) from interpretation.
  • Mark confidence: exploratory, provisional, supported (with caveats), or established (only when externally validated or exceptionally well evidenced).
  • When using generative or automated assistants, note where summaries were AI-assisted and verify citations against primary sources.

docs.title: Branch and naming conventions (suggested)
docs.text:
• main stays integration-ready; use short-lived branches: topic/<slug>, fix/<slug>, docs/<slug>.
  • Prefix issue titles for triage: [Hypothesis], [Methods], [Finding], [Docs], [Tooling].

docs.title: Outputs
docs.text:
• Place generated tables, figures, and exports that belong in version control under artifacts/ with a short README note on how they were produced.
  • Prefer deterministic scripts in scripts/ over one-off undocumented steps.

docs.title: Onboarding
docs.text: Path through the repository in ~30 minutes for a new collaborator or maintainer.

docs.title: 0. Optional — visual map (2 min)
docs.text: If you have a local clone, open the contributor hub at dashboard/index.html (see dashboard/README.md: run python -m http.server 8765 from the repo root, then /dashboard/). It mirrors this onboarding path with links to every step; use this document as the authoritative checklist.

docs.title: 1. Orientation (5 min)
docs.text:
Read VISION_AND_SCOPE.md and METHODOLOGY.md. You should be able to answer:

  • What is in scope for this repository?
  • What is the difference between a hypothesis, a finding, and speculation?

docs.title: 2. Integrity and data (5 min)
docs.text:
Read ETHICS_REPRODUCIBILITY_AND_DATA.md. Confirm you understand:

  • Which data classes may be committed
  • Why data/raw/ is gitignored

docs.title: 3. How we work together (5 min)
docs.text: Read COLLABORATION_AND_REVIEWS.md and CONTRIBUTING.md. Skim LABELS_AND_MILESTONES.md for triage vocabulary.

docs.title: 4. Traceability (5 min)
docs.text: Open DOC_MAP.md and scan REPOSITORY_MANIFEST.md. These explain which file enforces which policy.

docs.title: 5. Tooling (5 min)
docs.text:
• CI: .github/workflows/markdown-link-check.yml — link validation on PRs to main.
  • Dependabot: .github/dependabot.yml — keeps GitHub Actions references current.
  • Agents: If you use Cursor or other assistants, read AGENTS.md and .cursor/rules/science-discovery-core.mdc.
  • Spec-driven milestones (optional): GSD / get-shit-done — see GSD_INTEGRATION.md for how optional GSD use fits USDR policy and STATE.md.

docs.title: 6. Cadence (5 min)
docs.text: Read OPERATING_RHYTHM.md for weekly/monthly habits, tags, and branch protection expectations.

docs.title: Stream A — ship your first records (optional, 30–60 min)
docs.text:
After orientation, if you want a single concrete path (unknown YAML → hypothesis YAML → PR), follow HAPPY_PATH_FIRST_RECORDS.md.

Quality: QUALITY_BAR.md — how we keep structure, reviews, and CI from sliding into sloppiness.

docs.title: Optional: prompts
docs.text:
Try prompts/literature_synthesis.md or prompts/falsification_pass.md with your assistant when reviewing literature or a draft claim.

  • Custom assistant prompts: Keep project-specific briefings under docs/prompts/ next to the templates above when you add them.

docs.title: Where to put work
docs.text: You are adding… Put it in… Protocol / preregistration / design methods/ Allowed small data / manifests data/ Exploratory notebooks notebooks/ Reproducible scripts scripts/ Committed figure/table exports artifacts/

docs.title: When guiding documents change
docs.text:
1. Update DOC_MAP.md if behaviors shift.
  2. Sync AGENTS.md and .cursor/rules/ if agent-facing expectations changed.
  3. Sync issue/PR templates under .github/ if workflow fields need to match.
  4. Merge governance and tooling updates in one PR when possible so history stays coherent.
  5. Milestones and features: update CHANGELOG.md (Unreleased), README.md or deeper docs as needed, .planning/STATE.md, and dashboard/index.html so http://localhost:8765/dashboard/ stays accurate; run mkdocs build --strict when docs/ or mkdocs.yml changed. Cursor rule: .cursor/rules/documentation-and-dashboard.mdc.

docs.title: CI and merges
docs.text:
• Pull requests should pass Markdown link check before merge to main.
  • GitHub Pages: Until the published site at https://kr8zysho3.github.io/Universal-Science-Discovery/ returns HTTP 200, do not use that URL as a markdown hyperlink in tracked docs (the checker treats 404 as failure). Use backticks, or add/adjust an entry in .markdown-link-check.json ignorePatterns if you intentionally link before go-live.
  • Dependabot opens weekly PRs to update GitHub Actions; review and merge to reduce supply-chain drift.
  • Expand CI (lint, tests) when code under scripts/ grows.

docs.title: Versioning and tags
docs.text:
• Use date-based tags (YYYY.MM.DD or vYYYY.MM.summary) for snapshots of methodology or milestone reports when you need a citeable point in time.
  • Use semantic versioning (v1.2.0) only if the repo ships a reusable software artifact with API stability expectations.

docs.title: Releases
docs.text: • For major methodological or dataset snapshots described to external readers, attach release notes listing what changed and known limitations.

docs.title: Branch protection (GitHub settings)
docs.text:
Recommended for main once collaborators join:

  • Require pull request before merge.
  • Require status checks to pass — see Status check names below (pick what matches how strictly you want to gate merges).
  • Optionally require an approving review.

These options are configured under Settings → Branches → Branch protection rules in the GitHub UI; they are not stored in this repository.

docs.title: Status check names (as GitHub lists them)
docs.text:
Use Actions → … → workflow run to copy exact names into branch protection. Typical identifiers:

Workflow file Often appears as markdown-link-check.yml markdown-link-check (job: markdown-link-check) mkdocs-build.yml mkdocs-build / mkdocs validate-schemas.yml validate-schemas / validate-schemas ingest-ci.yml ingest tests / pytest-ingest

Ingest caveat: ingest-ci.yml runs only when PRs touch ingest-related paths (packages/ingest/, requirements-ingest.txt, envelope schema, etc.). If you mark ingest tests / pytest-ingest as required, GitHub may block merges on docs-only PRs where that workflow did not run. Options: omit it from required checks, use a ruleset that allows skipped optional checks, or touch an ingest path when you need the check to appear.

docs.title: Cadence
docs.text:
• Weekly (lightweight): triage issues, label stalled threads, skim open PRs.
  • Monthly: review whether docs/ still matches practice; prune obsolete prompts or scripts.
  • Per publication or external share: tag a snapshot and archive relevant artifacts with provenance.

docs.title: Quality bar — keeping USDR from going sloppy
docs.text: USDR fails when structure looks serious but records, evidence, and review drift into hand-waving. This page names the failure modes and the mitigations already wired (or to tighten) in the repo.

docs.title: What “sloppy” looks like here
docs.text: Failure mode Why it hurts YAML shape without substance u- / h- files that validate but say nothing falsifiable or well-scoped. Citation theater Lists of links without why they matter, or unverified AI-suggested DOIs/URLs. Bypassing checks Merging when validate_schemas / agreed CI is red, or “we’ll fix in follow-up” forever. Scope churn Rewriting schemas, paths, or happy path weekly so contributors never learn a stable loop. Drive-by docs README / hub / Stream A doc wrong versus what CI and schemas actually enforce. No reviewer lane Everything becomes “LGTM” aesthetic edits; nobody owns science integrity on content PRs.

docs.title: 1) Automated gates (objective)
docs.text:
• Schema validation: python scripts/validate_schemas.py on unknowns-catalog/** and hypotheses/** — failures are non-negotiable before merge.
  • CI on main PRs: markdown links, MkDocs strict build, schemas — keep required checks meaningful (see OPERATING_RHYTHM.md).
  • Ingest / tooling: separate path-filtered jobs — don’t block content PRs with optional infra checks, but don’t skip python -m pytest packages/ingest/tests when ingest changes.

docs.title: 2) Human gates (subjective but structured)
docs.text:
• Two-lane review (default posture):
  • Lane A — Shape: IDs, schema, LEGAL-safe links, file placement (fast, strict).
  • Lane B — Science: wording, falsifiability, evidence adequacy per METHODOLOGY.md (slower). Both can be one person on a tiny team, but call out which lane in review comments.
  • PR template: checklist is useless unless reviewers verify it; treat unchecked methodology/ethics boxes as needs discussion, not silent approval.
  • Small PRs: prefer one unknown or one hypothesis or one doc fix — not all three unless tightly coupled.

docs.title: 3) Stable “happy path”
docs.text: • HAPPY_PATH_FIRST_RECORDS.md is the single narrative for first records. Parallel work (Stream B, C, …) should not fork competing instructions; update the happy path when schemas or seed examples change.

docs.title: 4) Gold-standard exemplars (Stream B mental model)
docs.text:
• Treat unknowns-catalog/high-priority/u-dark-matter-microphysics.yaml and linked hypotheses as style references — not perfect science, but honest structure to imitate.
  • When a new record is better than seeds, consider adding a maintainer note or follow-up PR to align examples (avoid silent drift).

docs.title: 5) Doc / hub discipline
docs.text:
• Milestones or hub-affecting edits: follow .cursor/rules/documentation-and-dashboard.mdc — CHANGELOG, STATE, dashboard check at http://localhost:8765/dashboard/ when links are touched.
  • DOC_MAP.md / REPOSITORY_MANIFEST.md track “this file governs X”; if behavior changes, update traceability in the same PR.

docs.title: 6) Cadence and triage
docs.text:
• OPERATING_RHYTHM.md: weekly triage, monthly doc reality-check — prevents slow rot.
  • Labels and milestones: LABELS_AND_MILESTONES.md so “needs-evidence” and “blocked” mean something.

docs.title: Definition of “done” for a content PR
docs.text:
Minimum before merge:

  1. python scripts/validate_schemas.py exits 0 (or CI green equivalent).
  2. Links in the YAML comply with LEGAL.md (metadata / open access posture — no hosted paywalled full text).
  3. At least one review comment addresses Lane B if the PR makes or tightens scientific claims.
  4. If the PR changes contributor instructions or schemas, Stream A / templates / hub updated in the same merge bundle.

docs.title: Where to go deeper
docs.text:
• METHODOLOGY.md — evidence bar, claims discipline.
  • COLLABORATION_AND_REVIEWS.md — review expectations.
  • ETHICS_REPRODUCIBILITY_AND_DATA.md — data classes and integrity.
  • CONTRIBUTING.md — mechanics + AI use.

Bottom line: sloppiness is managed by objective CI + explicit review lanes + small PRs + stable happy path + living traceability docs — not by more ambition in ROADMAP.md alone.

docs.title: Repository manifest
docs.text:
Exhaustive index of tracked paths (excluding .git), what each is for, and which guiding documents govern it. Use this when onboarding, auditing, or changing structure.

ethics doc for sensitive data LICENSE Legal Default OSS license (MIT) LICENSING_NOTES.md .gitignore Config Files never committed ETHICS_REPRODUCIBILITY_AND_DATA.md .markdown-link-check.json Config Link checker rules for CI/local OPERATING_RHYTHM.md mkdocs.yml Config MkDocs Material site (sources under docs/) INTERFACE.md, OPERATING_RHYTHM.md, README.md requirements-docs.txt Config MkDocs / Material dependencies mkdocs.yml pyproject.toml Config Root pytest entry for packages/ingest/tests docs/DATA_PLAN.md requirements-ingest.txt Config Deps for usdr-ingest CLI docs/DATA_PLAN.md .cursor/rules/science-discovery-core.mdc Config Cursor always-on rules AGENTS.md, DOC_MAP.md .cursor/rules/agent-execution.mdc Config Cursor: prefer in-environment installs, tests, git AGENTS.md .cursor/rules/documentation-and-dashboard.mdc Config Cursor: keep docs, CHANGELOG, STATE, dashboard/ hub current; verify http://localhost:8765/dashboard/ when hub changes AGENTS.md, docs/OPERATING_RHYTHM.md, docs/DEV_DASHBOARD.md .github/dependabot.yml Config Dependency update PRs (Actions) OPERATING_RHYTHM.md .github/workflows/markdown-link-check.yml CI Validates Markdown links CONTRIBUTING.md, OPERATING_RHYTHM.md .github/workflows/validate-schemas.yml CI Validates hypothesis / unknown YAML against schemas/ schemas/README.md, OPERATING_RHYTHM.md .github/workflows/mkdocs-build.yml CI Builds MkDocs site from docs/ (mkdocs build --strict); uploads site artifact where configured INTERFACE.md, OPERATING_RHYTHM.md, mkdocs.yml .github/workflows/ingest-ci.yml CI Pytest for packages/ingest docs/DATA_PLAN.md, LEGAL.md .github/workflows/mkdocs-gh-pages.yml CI On push to main: mkdocs build --strict, deploy ./site to branch gh-pages (peaceiris/actions-gh-pages@v4). Live: https://kr8zysho3.github.io/Universal-Science-Discovery/ after Pages source is gh-pages / root INTERFACE.md, README.md .github/pull_request_template.md Template PR checklist METHODOLOGY.md, docs.title: Maintenance rule
docs.text: When you add, rename, or remove a policy-relevant path, update this manifest and DOC_MAP.md in the same change.

docs.title: User acceptance — usdr-ingest (pilot)
docs.text: Manual checks for the packages/ingest CLI. Automated contract is pytest packages/ingest/tests (fixtures + envelope schema). Policy: DATA_PLAN.md, LEGAL.md — metadata only, no PDFs or full text.

docs.title: Preconditions
docs.text:
From the repository root:

pip install -r requirements-ingest.txt
pip install -e "./packages/ingest[dev]"

docs.title: Automated (run before merge or release)
docs.text: python -m pytest packages/ingest/tests -q

docs.title: CLI smoke (no network)
docs.text:
• usdr-ingest --help — Typer help; harvest and version subcommands listed.
  • usdr-ingest harvest --help — OAI options, --dry-run, --max-records, --output, --manifest.
  • usdr-ingest version — prints installed package version.

docs.title: Optional live OAI check (network)
docs.text:
Use only if you accept minimal traffic to arXiv’s OAI endpoint and comply with current arXiv OAI / terms. Prefer --dry-run and --max-records 1 (or similarly small).

Example:

usdr-ingest harvest --dry-run --max-records 1 --from 2024-01-01 --until 2024-01-02

Expect stderr JSON with dry_run, base_url, and counters (list_requests, get_requests, etc.). To emit one JSONL envelope line to a local file, omit --dry-run, add e.g. -o sample.jsonl, inspect the line, then delete — do not commit ad hoc harvest output without maintainer review (ETHICS_REPRODUCIBILITY_AND_DATA.md).

Default OAI base is HTTPS export.arxiv.org/oai (see packages/ingest); override only when testing against a documented endpoint.

docs.title: Envelope validation
docs.text: Each output line MUST validate against schemas/ingestion-envelope-1.0.0.json. Tests cover this; for ad hoc lines use your JSON Schema tool of choice or extend pytest locally.

docs.title: Vision
docs.text: Universal Science Discovery is a disciplined home for cross-domain scientific exploration: hypotheses, evidence, methods, and reproducible artifacts live together with clear separation between what we believe, what we have shown, and what we are trying to falsify.

docs.title: In scope
docs.text:
• Documented hypotheses, methods, protocols, and analysis notes.
  • Scripts, notebooks, and small tools that support reproducibility.
  • Summaries of literature and structured argument maps where they inform active threads.
  • Versioned non-sensitive datasets or references to external datasets with provenance.

docs.title: Out of scope (for this repository)
docs.text:
• Raw restricted or identifiable human data committed to git.
  • Undocumented “final truth” claims without methods and limitations.
  • Production services, secrets, or credentials.

docs.title: Active threads
docs.text: Track active hypotheses and investigation threads in GitHub Issues using the finding review template or a dedicated label (see COLLABORATION_AND_REVIEWS.md). Optionally maintain a short index in README once threads multiply.

docs.title: Terminology
docs.text:
• Hypothesis: A testable statement; not yet established.
  • Finding: A conclusion tied to explicit evidence and methods; may be overturned.
  • Speculation: Clearly labeled exploration; must not be presented as findings.

docs.title: Documentation examples
docs.text:
Small, checked-in artifacts that illustrate repository contracts without pulling live external data.

File Purpose arxiv-oai-metadata-sample.v1.jsonl Two synthetic envelope rows matching schemas/ingestion-envelope-1.0.0.json. Content is derived from the same OAI XML fixtures used in packages/ingest tests, not from a live arXiv harvest.

Traceability: DATA_PLAN.md (Phase A optional exemplar), LEGAL.md (metadata-only scope).

To re-generate the sample from fixtures (optional maintainer refresh), run from the repo root with packages/ingest installed:

python scripts/generate-ingest-example-jsonl.py

Then replace retrieved_at with fixed RFC 3339 timestamps so the file stays deterministic in Git.

docs.title: Bridge discovery prompt
docs.text: Use this prompt when reading a paper or working through a topic to systematically check whether the phenomenon being studied already has an analogue in another field — and whether the two communities know about each other.

docs.title: The prompt
docs.text:
I am reading a paper / working on [TOPIC] in the field of [FIELD A].

Help me identify whether this phenomenon, model, or result has a close analogue in
one or more other scientific fields, where the fields may be using different language,
different experimental tools, or different mathematics to study what is fundamentally
the same underlying system or process.

For each candidate analogue field [FIELD B]:

1. BRIDGE CLAIM
   State precisely what concept, model, or result in [FIELD A] maps onto what concept,
   model, or result in [FIELD B]. Be specific about the mapping — do not just say
   "both study complexity."

2. TRANSLATION TABLE
   List 3–5 pairs of terms where one field's vocabulary corresponds to the other's,
   and explain why the correspondence is non-trivial (i.e. not just a synonym, but a
   genuine structural equivalence).

3. WHAT EACH FIELD HAS THAT THE OTHER LACKS
   - What tools / data / theory does [FIELD A] have that [FIELD B] needs?
   - What tools / data / theory does [FIELD B] have that [FIELD A] needs?

4. CROSS-POLLINATION OPPORTUNITY
   Describe one specific experiment, collaboration, or computation that becomes
   possible — or much more tractable — when both fields share knowledge.

5. COMMUNICATION GAP
   Why haven't these fields already converged? Name the specific barriers:
   journal siloing, incompatible training, grant structure, data format mismatch,
   terminology, etc.

6. STATUS ASSESSMENT
   Is this connection:
   - proposed  — plausible but not yet documented in cross-disciplinary literature
   - established — recognised in at least one explicit cross-disciplinary paper
   - contested — active disagreement about whether the analogy holds
   - stale — the fields have diverged or the connection was refuted

Rate your confidence in each candidate analogue on a scale of 1–5.

docs.title: How to use this output
docs.text:
Once you have responses, record the highest-confidence bridges as YAML entries under cross-domain/. A bridge entry requires at minimum:

Field Content id b-<field-a>-<field-b> or b-<short-descriptive-name> title One sentence stating the equivalence status proposed / established / contested / stale fields At least two disciplines bridge_claim The precise mapping, 2–5 sentences

Then run python scripts/validate_schemas.py to confirm the file is valid.

docs.title: Heuristics for spotting bridge candidates
docs.text:
These patterns often signal an unrecognised cross-domain connection:

Signal Example Power-law / scale-free behaviour Zipf in language, Pareto in economics, scale-free networks in biology — often the same universality class Phase transitions and critical points Any field that describes a sharp qualitative change as a parameter crosses a threshold Conservation laws When two fields have conserved quantities with the same mathematical structure Network topology Graph-theoretic results (percolation, small-world, modularity) apply wherever entities connect Optimisation under constraints Evolution, economics, and engineering all find optima under resource constraints — the mathematics is shared Self-organisation Whenever a system develops structure without a central controller Information and entropy Shannon entropy appears in thermodynamics, biology, linguistics, and finance Oscillators and synchronisation Kuramoto model applies to neurons, fireflies, power grids, and financial markets

docs.title: Example: How the existing bridges were found
docs.text:
Session: Reading arXiv 2504.18362 on percolation in active solids.

Bridge candidate: statistical physics ↔ oncology

Bridge claim: The percolation threshold in a passive bond network is the same mathematical object as the minimum viable vascular density in a tumour under adaptive therapy. Active matter physics adds a new wrinkle — self-propulsion can drive a network below its threshold paradoxically.

Result: b-percolation-oncology (existing entry) strengthened; new unknown u-active-matter-percolation opened from the physics side.

See cross-domain/ for the full catalog of existing bridges.

docs.title: Prompt: falsification / red-team pass
docs.text:
Use before promoting work to finding status (see METHODOLOGY.md).

Proposed claim or finding: [one paragraph]

Supporting evidence (summary): [bullets + links to methods/data]

Instructions:

  1. List the strongest reasons this claim could be wrong, including boring base-rate explanations.
  2. Identify confounders, selection effects, and leakage if any ML/statistics are involved.
  3. Propose alternative models that fit the same observations.
  4. Suggest checks (robustness, ablations, independent replication paths) ranked by feasibility.
  5. Rate overall resilience of the claim: fragile / moderate / robust—with caveats.

Output format: “How I’d try to break this” as a numbered list, then “What would change my mind.”

docs.title: Prompt: hypothesis comparison
docs.text:
Use with METHODOLOGY.md and VISION_AND_SCOPE.md.

Hypotheses:

  • H1: [statement]
  • H2: [competing statement]

Tasks:

  1. Restate each hypothesis in falsifiable form. List observable implications that differ between H1 and H2.
  2. For each hypothesis, list existing evidence (with citations) and missing evidence.
  3. Propose decisive or incremental experiments (conceptual or concrete) that would shift belief—with expected costs/risks.
  4. State which hypothesis is more parsimonious and in what domain that parsimony applies.
  5. Explicitly list what you would need to admit you were wrong for the currently preferred hypothesis.

Constraints: Label speculation. Do not upgrade speculative reasoning to “findings.”

docs.title: Prompt: literature synthesis (methodology-aligned)
docs.text:
Use this with your assistant after reading METHODOLOGY.md. Replace bracketed fields.

Context: I am investigating [thread / hypothesis summary]. Primary questions: [Q1], [Q2].

Instructions:

  1. Summarize only claims that are directly supported by the sources I provide or that you can cite with stable identifiers (DOI, arXiv ID, journal volume/issue). Flag anything you cannot cite as uncited and do not treat it as established.
  2. For each source, give: core claim relevant to my questions, methods (brief), and limitations.
  3. End with an evidence table: claim → source → strength of evidence (exploratory / mixed / strong) → what would falsify or weaken the claim.
  4. Separate community consensus (if any) from contested areas.
  5. Note any conflicts of interest or funding bias mentioned in the sources.

Sources (paste links or references):

[paste here]
